coredata.dc:description: The design and theoretical study of green, low-cost heterojunction photocatalysts with strong interfacial interactions are crucial for achieving efficient and stable photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (PHE). This study proposes a simple method to synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/NaNbO3 heterojunction with dynamic oxygen vacancies. GQDs promote the formation of oxygen vacancies and increase the surface-active sites of NaNbO3, significantly enhancing light absorption efficiency. This results in superior separation of photogenerated charge carriers in the GQDs/NaNbO3 composite and improves PHE activity and stability. Under the reaction conditions of methanol as a sacrificial agent, the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst exhibited the highest hydrogen production rate of 775.9 μmol·g−1·h−1. Notably, in cyclic stability tests, the catalyst demonstrated higher catalytic activity in subsequent cycles compared to the initial cycle. The activity of the second round was 1.5 times that of the first round, and the hydrogen evolution rate was 1204.9 μmol·g−1·h−1. This improvement is likely attributed to the fast electron transport channel between NaNbO3 and GQDs, which facilitated the transfer of photo-generated electrons from NaNbO3 to GQDs, thereby promoting the generation of dynamic oxygen vacancies and improving the PHE performance. Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analyses confirmed the crucial role of dynamic oxygen vacancies in enhancing catalytic activity. Furthermore, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations and experimental results elucidated the charge transfer mechanism and PHE process. This study provides valuable insights for the design of efficient and durable photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design and theoretical study of green, low-cost heterojunction photocatalysts with strong interfacial interactions are crucial for achieving efficient and stable photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (PHE). This study proposes a simple method to synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/NaNbO3 heterojunction with dynamic oxygen vacancies. GQDs promote the formation of oxygen vacancies and increase the surface-active sites of NaNbO3, significantly enhancing light absorption efficiency. This results in superior separation of photogenerated charge carriers in the GQDs/NaNbO3 composite and improves PHE activity and stability. Under the reaction conditions of methanol as a sacrificial agent, the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst exhibited the highest hydrogen production rate of 775.9 μmol·g−1·h−1. Notably, in cyclic stability tests, the catalyst demonstrated higher catalytic activity in subsequent cycles compared to the initial cycle. The activity of the second round was 1.5 times that of the first round, and the hydrogen evolution rate was 1204.9 μmol·g−1·h−1. This improvement is likely attributed to the fast electron transport channel between NaNbO3 and GQDs, which facilitated the transfer of photo-generated electrons from NaNbO3 to GQDs, thereby promoting the generation of dynamic oxygen vacancies and improving the PHE performance. Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analyses confirmed the crucial role of dynamic oxygen vacancies in enhancing catalytic activity. Furthermore, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations and experimental results elucidated the charge transfer mechanism and PHE process. This study provides valuable insights for the design of efficient and durable photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, environmental degradation and the increasing scarcity of natural resources have posed unprecedented challenges to human survival and societal development. Among the most pressing global issues are energy shortages and climate change, which have garnered growing attention from scientists worldwide [1,2]. As non-renewable energy sources, such as fossil fuels, continue to deplete, the exploration and development of novel, sustainable energy sources has become essential for addressing the energy crisis and achieving sustainable energy development [3]. Hydrogen (H2) energy, a clean, zero-carbon, and highly efficient secondary energy source, has emerged as a promising solution to mitigate the global energy crisis. It plays a crucial role in advancing the objectives of the global 'Race to Zero' initiative. H2 energy is highly valued for its potential to address global energy challenges, owing to its high energy density, diverse range of sources, and abundant reserves. However, conventional hydrogen production methods, such as fossil fuel reforming and coal gasification, contradict the primary objective of mitigating environmental and energy challenges [4–6]. Therefore, advancing renewable energy technologies for hydrogen production is essential. Among these, solar-driven PHE presents a promising, efficient approach. The development of advanced, high-performance semiconductor catalysts is crucial for improving the efficiency of PHE processes. Despite the development of various catalysts being explored for PHE (e.g., TiO2 [7], CdS [8], g-C3N4 [9], ZnIn2S4 [10], etc.), several challenges remain. These include inefficient charge carrier generation, low solar energy utilization, and suboptimal photocatalytic activity and stability, which continue to impede the industrial-scale implementation of hydrogen production. Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop cost-effective, highly efficient PHE catalysts with enhanced activity and long-term stability.

Among various photocatalysts, NaNbO3 stands out due to its perovskite structure, high absorption coefficient, exceptional chemical stability, superior crystallinity, and minimal environmental impact, making it a promising candidate for photocatalysis [11]. However, the slow kinetics and high photogenerated carrier recombination rate caused by the wide band gap (Eg ≈ 3.4 eV) of NaNbO3 severely restrict its photocatalytic activity [12,13]. In order to overcome the inherent limitations of NaNbO3 and enhance its photocatalytic efficiency and versatility, researchers have employed several innovative strategies, including morphological control, elemental doping, oxygen vacancy manipulation, noble metal incorporation, and heterojunction construction [14–17]. These strategies are specifically designed to target and improve certain properties of NaNbO3, thereby significantly boosting its photocatalytic performance. Among them, oxygen vacancy engineering plays a critical role by regulating charge transport properties and optimizing the electronic structure of semiconductor materials. It achieves this by incorporating localized states as charge carrier trapping centers within the band gap. This approach also adjusts the adsorption energy and modifies the reaction pathways of intermediate species, thereby enhancing photocatalyst performance [18,19]. Zhao et al. [16] synthesized oxygen-vacancy-rich NaNbO3 using an ion-exchange method, which adjusted the energy band structure due to the presence of oxygen vacancies. This adjustment enhanced electron reduction and facilitated charge separation, leading to improved photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance. Yang et al. [20] engineered NaNbO3 with a smaller band gap, larger specific surface area, and higher charge density through oxygen vacancy engineering, resulting in enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity. However, oxygen vacancies are inherently unstable due to their short lifetime in the presence of air, impacting the surface chemistry and reducing the stability of semiconductor materials [21]. The presence of oxygen vacancies on the surface of semiconductor materials introduces significant instability, resulting in reduced reactivity and inherent hindrance to photocatalytic reactions [22]. Since oxygen vacancies may be filled with oxygen-containing reactants during long-term stability tests, the cyclic catalytic stability can be significantly enhanced if oxygen vacancies are dynamically regenerated before each cycle test. Thus, employing a dynamic oxygen vacancy strategy is crucial for further improving catalytic stability [23]. Dong et al. [24] identified the dynamic evolution of active sites on defective BiOCl catalysts in gas–solid photocatalytic reactions through in situ characterization techniques. Their findings revealed that photoexcited dynamic oxygen vacancies served as active sites for the photocatalytic reaction, facilitating the conversion of reaction intermediates into products. Similarly, Sun et al. [25] employed in situ photoinduced modulation of the structural properties of oxygen vacancies in BiSbO4, enabling the regeneration of oxygen vacancies as they were consumed. This approach achieved dynamic stabilization of oxygen vacancies, enhancing both the effectiveness and stability of formaldehyde photocatalytic oxidation. Therefore, developing novel photocatalysts with dynamic oxygen vacancies appears to be a promising strategy for achieving high activity and long-term stability in photocatalytic reactions. However, relying solely on the oxygen vacancy strategy cannot fully address the key challenges of limited photo response range and low photocatalytic efficiency.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have garnered significant attention in energy, catalysis, and optics due to their cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, robust stability, and excellent physicochemical properties [26]. These materials have also demonstrated the ability to enhance light absorption, improve photocatalytic activity, and stabilize oxygen vacancies [27]. GQDs, a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, possess abundant surface functional groups and exhibit unique quantum confinement effects, making them highly promising for PHE [28,29]. For instance, Min et al. [30] utilized GQDs as electron acceptors and covalently functionalized them onto the surface of CdS to fabricate CdS/GQDs nanohybrids, which exhibited remarkable visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution capabilities. Similarly, Dong et al. [31] designed a GQDs-modified covalent organic framework with a visible light H2 yield of 487 μmol·g−1·h−1. In this system, GQDs acted as photosensitizers and electron acceptors, effectively inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated carriers and improving visible-light absorption. In conclusion, GQDs significantly enhance the activity and stability of photocatalysts by facilitating the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers, thereby leading to overall improvements in photocatalytic performance. These findings not only provide theoretical and empirical support for the use of GQDs in PHE, but also introduce new concepts and methodologies for developing innovative, high-performance photocatalysts. Inspired by this analysis, designing a GQDs-modified, oxygen-rich vacancy NaNbO3 catalyst for efficient PHE reaction is of great significance. Additionally, investigating the reaction mechanism of GQDs in this process is crucial.

In this study, GQDs/NaNbO3 nanocomposites with dynamic oxygen vacancies were successfully synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method combined with ultrasonic self-assembly to enhance PHE reactivity and stability. The fundamental characteristics of the GQDs/NaNbO3 composite, determined through XRD, XPS, and ESR analysis, as well as UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy, photochemical assessments, and free radical investigations, revealed that GQDs act as effective electron acceptors and reaction centers. They increase the oxygen vacancy concentration and surface-active sites of NaNbO3, while enhancing the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes during PHE reaction. Moreover, the photogenerated carrier transfer pathways and the reaction mechanisms at the interface between GQDs and NaNbO3 were investigated using various characterization techniques and DFT calculations. Compared to other typical catalysts (eg., TiO2), the synergistic effect of non-noble metal modification and dynamic oxygen vacancies in GQDs/NaNbO3 exhibited unique PHE activity and stability (Table S1). This study provides valuable insights into the development of highly efficient NaNbO3-based photocatalysts, which could play a crucial role in addressing current energy and environmental challenges.

Niobium chloride (NbCl5, 99.9%) was procured from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-ChemTechnologyCo., Ltd. Glycerol (C3H8O3, AR), isopropanol (IPA, AR), nitric acid (HNO3), triethanolamine (TEOA, C6H15NO3, AR), ethylene glycol (EG, (CH2OH)2, AR), lactic acid (C3H6O3, AR) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pyrene (C16H10, 98%), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, AR) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, AR) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

GQDs were synthesized based on the method previously described by Pan et al. [32], with necessary modifications to meet specific experimental requirements. Briefly, 1 g pyrene was nitrated in 80 ml hot HNO3 at 160 °C, producing 1,3,6-trinitropyrene. Subsequently, 0.02 g of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was added to 10 mL of 0.2 M NaOH solution and dispersed via ultrasonication for 2 h. The suspension was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the water-soluble GQDs were obtained by filtering the product through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The filtrate was further purified by dialysis in a dialysis bag for 48 h, resulting in a purified GQD solution (∼0.7 mg/ml).

The synthesis of NaNbO3 composite material involves two sequential steps. First, Nb2O5 microspheres were prepared using the method reported by Mandri et al. [33]. Subsequently, NaNbO3 nanocrystals were synthesized via a previously optimized solvothermal method [34]. In brief, 15 mL of IPA was combined with 15 mL of glycerol and stirred for 30 min, followed by the addition of 0.45 g of NbCl5 with continued agitation for an additional 90 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave, where the reaction was conducted at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed several times with deionized water and ethanol. The precipitate was then dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C and calcined at 600 °C for 3 h to obtain Nb2O5 microspheres. Subsequently, 0.6 g of Nb2O5 microspheres were dispersed in 56 mL of EG along with 8 mL of a 5 M NaOH solution. After vigorous ultrasonication for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 140 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the product was thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol, then dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C.

The GQDs/NaNbO3 composite materials were synthesized via a self-assembly process involving freeze-drying and ultrasonic agitation. In general, 0.2 g of NaNbO3 nanocrystals were dispersed in 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Subsequently, varying amounts of GQDs aqueous solution were added, followed by ultrasound treatment in an ice bath for 1 h and stirring for 12 h to ensure uniform dispersion. The resulting mixture was washed with deionized water, frozen in liquid nitrogen, and freeze-dried for 24 h. After freeze-drying, GQDs/NaNbO3 nanocomposites were obtained. GQDs were incorporated into NaNbO3 at mass ratios of x:100, where x was 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0. The resulting samples were denoted as x%GQDs/NaNbO3, and the potential formation mechanism is depicted in Scheme 1 .

In order to elucidate the crystal structures, physical phases, and chemical compositions of the materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using an X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu S-7000) at 40 kV and 30 mA with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm). Raman spectra were obtained on a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA JY LabRAM HR Evolution) with a TEM00 laser (532 nm). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired with an FT-IR spectrometer (BRUKER TENSOR 27). The morphology, surface characteristics, and elemental distribution of the catalyst were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300) at 10.0 kV and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G20), combined with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) measurements at 200.0 kV. Oxygen vacancy signals were detected using an electron spin resonance (ESR, JES-FA300) spectrometer. The elemental composition and electronic valence states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha) with an Al/Mg double anode as the X-ray source and a monochromatic Al Kα as the emission source (1486.6 eV), using C 1s (284.8 eV) for reference calibration. Ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, Agilent Cary-100) was employed to measure optical absorption properties using an integrating sphere over the wavelength range of 200–800 nm, with BaSO4 as a reference. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLS-1000). The main active species (including h+, O2 − and OH) generated on the catalyst surface were captured using an ESR spectrometer with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO-methanol solution) or 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperonyl (TEMPO water solution) as spin trapping agents at room temperature under simulated sunlight.

The photoelectrochemical properties were assessed through transient photocurrent density (TPCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments using an electrochemical workstation (Chinstruments CHI660E). The control experiment was conducted in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. A thin-film working electrode coated with the photocatalyst was prepared on FTO conductive glass. Platinum wire served as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode. TPCD measurements were taken under the illumination of a 300 W Xenon lamp. EIS was performed at a direct current potential of 0.2 V relative to the open circuit potential, with a frequency range from 1000 Hz to 1 MHz.

PHE reactions were conducted using an all-glass automatic online trace gas analysis system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.). A 300 W Xenon lamp (HSX-F300, Beijing NEBT Technology Co., Ltd.) served as the simulated sunlight source. The photocatalyst (100 mg) was uniformly dispersed in 100 mL methanol solution (V(methanol):V(water) = 10:90) with a magnetic stirrer, and the reaction temperature was maintained at 25 °C using a water condenser. Before the PHE reaction, the reaction system was completely degassed to eliminate any dissolved gases. Hydrogen production was analyzed in-line using a gas chromatograph (GC-9790Ⅱ, Zhejiang Fuli Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd., China) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a 5 Å molecular sieve column with argon as the carrier gas. Photostability was evaluated by assessing the photocatalytic activity over five consecutive cycles. After each 5 h cycle of PHE, the photocatalyst was separated from the reaction system, dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and then cooled to room temperature to obtain the recovered photocatalyst for subsequent testing. Additionally, under identical conditions, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst was evaluated using bandpass filters (λ = 350 nm, 365 nm). The corresponding light intensities (I) were 365.4 mW/cm2 and 269.9 mW/cm2, respectively. The AQE was calculated using the following formula [35]:
AQE =
2 × n
(
H 2
)
×
N A
× h × c
I × t × λ × A
where n(H2) is the amount of H2 generated per unit time, NA is Avogadro constant, h is Planck constant, c is the speed of light, t is the reaction time, λ is the incident wavelength, and A is the incident light area (50.3 cm2).

Our DFT calculations were conducted using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with plane-wave basis sets and the projector augmented-wave (PAW) method [36–39]. The exchange–correlation potential was treated with the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) parametrization [40]. Grimme’s DFT-D3 model was applied for van der Waals corrections. The energy cutoff was set to 450 eV [41]. The NaNbO3 (100) surface was modeled using a 3 × 3 × 1 supercell, consisting of four atomic layers. The atomic positions of the top two layers were optimized, while the bottom two layers were fixed. A vacuum space of 15 Å was introduced to prevent periodic interactions. The Brillouin-zone integration was sampled using a Γ-centered Monkhorst-Pack mesh of 2 × 2 × 1 [42]. The structures were fully relaxed until the maximum force on each atom was less than 0.02 eV/Å, and the energy convergence criterion was set to 10−5 eV. The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) was calculated using the computational hydrogen electrode (CHE) model proposed by Nørskov et al. [43,44]. The reaction-free energy is defined as the difference between the initial and final states, as shown in the following equation: ΔG = ΔE + ΔZPE − TΔS + ΔGU + ΔGpH.

Among them, ΔE is the adsorption energy of the reaction intermediate calculated by DFT, ΔZPE is the zero point energy (ZPE), the temperature T is set to 298.15 K, and ΔS is the entropy change. In addition, U is the contribution to the free energy relative to the standard hydrogen electrode. ΔGpH is the correction value of hydrogen ion free energy with concentration.

The XRD analysis was conducted to examine the crystal structure and phase purity of the samples. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the characteristic peaks of pure NaNbO3 were located at 22.75°, 32.39°, 46.46°, 52.33°, and 57.77° corresponding to (1 0 0), (1 1 0), (2 0 0), (2 1 0), and (2 1 1) crystal planes of cubic NaNbO3 (JCPDS No. 75-2102). These peaks match the cubic perovskite structure of the Pm-3m (221) space group, with a lattice parameter of a = 3.906(1) Å. The metastable cubic structure of perovskite NaNbO3 crystals, characterized by high structural symmetry, facilitates more efficient electron transfer compared to the orthorhombic structure [34]. The doping of GQDs did not significantly alter the NaNbO3 structure compared to the pure phase, and no discernible signals for GQDs were observed in the XRD pattern of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3. This absence may be attributed to the low diffraction intensity from the small quantity of GQDs, their low crystallinity, and homogeneous dispersion on NaNbO3. No impurity phases were detected at this resolution. Furthermore, Raman spectroscopy was used to further investigate crystallinity, disorder, defects, and chemical modifications in the hybrid materials. As shown in Fig. 1(b), both NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 samples exhibited characteristic Raman bands of perovskite-type NaNbO3, with a distinct peak at 233 cm−1 attributed to the Na translational vibrational mode. The peak at 600 cm−1 corresponds to varying Nb-O-Nb bond lengths in the stretching mode of NbO6 octahedra [45]. In particular, pure GQDs exhibited two distinct peaks at 1322 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the characteristic Raman D and G bands of GQDs, which arise from the in-plane vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and the edge states of graphene [46,47]. In the Raman spectrum of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3, both NaNbO3 and GQD peaks were present, with the GQD peaks slightly shifted, indicating a strong interaction between the two components. The XRD and Raman results tentatively confirmed the presence of GQDs in the composite. Additionally, Fig. 1(c) depicts the FT-IR spectra of NaNbO3 and the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 composite [48]. The vibrational bands between 500–800 cm−1 correspond to the Nb-O bonding in NaNbO3 phase. The OH vibrational band at 3420 cm−1 becomes more pronounced in 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3, likely due to lattice distortions caused by Nb-O bond formation or the rearrangement of oxygen vacancies during hybridization. More importantly, the broad peaks attributed to OH vibration became broader and weaker due to the hydrogen bonding interactions between NaNbO3 and GQDs [46,49]. Notably, in addition to the CC vibration at 1630 cm−1, two weak vibrations at 1265 cm−1 and 1185 cm−1 correspond to the C-OH vibration in the GQDs/NaNbO3 spectrum, which indicates that the hydroxyl functional of GQDs present on the surface of NaNbO3 [50]. The appearance of these additional vibrational peaks in the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 spectra confirms the successful modification of NaNbO3 by GQDs.

To comprehensively investigate the microstructure and morphological characteristics of the GQDs/NaNbO3 samples, SEM, HRTEM, and elemental mapping techniques were employed. Fig. S1 shows the high crystallinity and irregular, grain-like morphology of NaNbO3. The surface of NaNbO3 particles exhibited uneven topography, with noticeable voids within the grain structure and relatively large particle sizes. After modification with GQDs, the crystallinity and morphology of the samples remain largely unchanged. The TEM images (Fig. 2 (a)) and the corresponding size distribution inset revealed that GQDs have an average size of 4–5 nm, with consistent and homogeneous dispersion in aqueous solution. Fig. 2(b) showed a clear interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the crystal plane of GQDs [51]. In Fig. 2(c) and (d), lattice spacings of 0.39 nm and 0.22 nm were observed, corresponding to the (110) crystal plane of NaNbO3 and the (002) plane of GQDs, respectively. The successful modification of GQDs on the NaNbO3 surface was further supported by the XRD and Raman results. To confirm this modification, elemental mapping analysis was also performed. The elemental mapping indicated that Na, Nb, O and C elements were uniformly distributed across the surface of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 photocatalyst.

In order to demonstrate the presence of oxygen vacancies in the samples, ESR analysis was performed on different samples, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). Pure NaNbO3 exhibited a weak signal at g = 2.001, attributed to oxygen vacancy resonance. Notably, the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 sample displayed a significantly stronger oxygen vacancy signal at g = 2.001, indicating that the incorporation of GQDs increases the oxygen vacancy concentration [52]. XPS analysis was performed to further investigate the chemical composition and valence states of the NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 composites. As shown in Fig. S2, the XPS survey spectra of the sample displayed prominent Na 1s, Nb 3d, O 1s, and C 1s signals. The Na 1s, Nb 3d, and O 1s signals originated from NaNbO3, while the C 1s signal confirmed the presence of GQDs in the composite. Fig. 3(b) showed the high-resolution C 1s spectra of NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3, with peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.6 eV corresponding to CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively [46]. Fig. 3(c) presented the high-resolution O 1s spectra of NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3. The XPS spectra of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 displayed three characteristic peaks in the O 1s region at 533.7 eV, 531.4 eV, and 529.7 eV. The sequential shift in binding energies from higher to lower values indicated the presence of surface-adsorbed oxygen, dangling bonds (OH), and lattice oxygen, respectively. Notably, the presence of dangling bonds in the O 1s spectra indicated the formation of oxygen vacancies [53]. The relative concentration of surface oxygen vacancies could be quantitatively estimated by comparing the relative intensity ratios of lattice oxygen to the O 1s peaks of dangling bonds and adsorbed oxygen. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the oxygen vacancy concentration value of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 (19.98%) exhibited a significant increase compared to that of NaNbO3 (16.40%), indicating a higher concentration of oxygen vacancies on the surface of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 [16]. This result was consistent with the findings from the ESR analysis. Additionally, the Nb 3d3/2 and Nb 3d5/2 peaks for both NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 photocatalysts were observed at approximately 209.4 eV and 206.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 3(d)), confirming the presence of Nb in the +5 oxidation state, consistent with previous studies on NaNbO3 [54]. Interestingly, the peaks in both the high-resolution Nb 3d and O 1s spectra of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 composite shift to higher binding energies, suggesting a strong interaction between NaNbO3 and GQDs. This shift may result from electron transfer, with NaNbO3 donating electrons to GQDs at the interface. The aforementioned studies demonstrated that the incorporation of GQDs not only increased the oxygen vacancy concentration on the NaNbO3 surface but also improved the photocatalytic performance of the composite through electron transfer between NaNbO3 and GQDs.

The light absorption properties of the GQDs/NaNbO3 composites were examined using UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), pure NaNbO3 exhibits no absorption in the visible region, with significant absorption primarily in the UV region, and an absorption edge around 350 nm. The GQDs/NaNbO3 sample changes color from white to light brown due to the combined effects of GQDs and oxygen vacancy defects. The band edge of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 exhibited a redshift compared to that of NaNbO3. These results indicated that the strong interaction between GQDs and NaNbO3 enhances surface coupling, thereby improving the light absorption capacity of NaNbO3, extending the absorption edge, and reducing the bandgap. These modifications facilitate the generation of photogenerated charge carriers under light irradiation, enhance the separation and migration of interfacial charges, and ultimately improve the photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/NaNbO3 composite material [46,55]. Due to the low loading capacity of GQDs, the absorption peak of GQDs is not displayed in 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 composite catalyst. Fig. S3 presents the UV–vis DRS spectra of NaNbO3 samples doped with varying amounts of GQDs. With increasing GQD doping, a more pronounced redshift in the absorption edge was observed compared to the pure NaNbO3 phase. However, these subtle shifts had minimal impact on the overall light absorption capacity. In addition, the Kubelka-Munk equation was used to calculate the band gap (Eg) values of NaNbO3, 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 and GQDs from their respective UV–vis DRS spectra.

= A
( h v -

)

Here, α represents the absorption coefficient, ℎν denotes the incident photon energy, n is the exponent determined by the type of semiconductor, and A is a proportionality constant specific to the material [56,57]. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the Eg of NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 were approximately 3.35 eV and 3.19 eV, respectively. The 0.16 eV reduction in the band gap of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 compared to pure NaNbO3 can be attributed to the incorporation of GQDs [58]. Subsequently, Mott-Schottky (M–S) analysis was conducted to evaluate the flat band potential (EFB) of catalysts. The observed positive slopes indicated n-type semiconductor behavior (Fig. 4(c–d)). The measured EFB values for NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 were −0.95 eV and −0.93 eV, respectively. For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential is typically located near the conduction band (CB) edge, ranging from approximately 0.1–0.2 eV. Typically, there is a potential difference of 0.2 eV between the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) and the Ag/AgCl reference electrode, where EFB is related by the expression: φ(NHE) = φ(Ag/AgCl) + 0.2 eV [59]. Accordingly, the estimated conduction band edge positions for NaNbO3, GQDs, and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 were −0.75 eV and −0.73 eV, respectively. Based on the following equation: EVB = ECB + Eg, the valence band (VB) edge positions for these materials were calculated to be 2.60 eV and 2.46 eV, respectively.

Based on the aforementioned characteristic analysis, the PHE performance of the synthesized materials (NaNbO3, x%GQDs/NaNbO3) was evaluated under simulated solar light irradiation with methanol as the sacrificial reagents. As depicted in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), Pure GQDs showed no PHE activity, and pristine NaNbO3 exhibited poor PHE performance, producing only 2593.5 μmol·g−1 of H2 after 5 h of reaction. However, the introduction of GQDs into NaNbO3 significantly improved the catalyst's PHE performance. Among the composites, 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 exhibited the highest PHE activity, achieving 3879.5 μmol·g−1 of H2 after 5 h of irradiation, a 1.5-times increase compared to pure NaNbO3. However, the PHE performance of the catalyst declined with the increased loading of GQDs. At 3% GQDs, the catalytic activity of 3%GQDs/NaNbO3 was inferior to that of pure NaNbO3. As the doping level of GQDs increases, a thicker GQD layer may form within the nanoparticle heterojunction, or GQDs may aggregate, hindering direct contact with NaNbO3 nanoparticles, which could result in decreased catalytic activity [60]. Additionally, the aggregation of GQDs may impair the absorption of incident light by NaNbO3 nanoparticles, potentially reducing the catalyst's performance. Fig. 5(c) shows the hydrogen production activity of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 in different sacrificial reagents, indicating that the sample exhibits lower hydrogen production activity in the lactic acid and Na2S/Na2SO3 systems, which is significantly lower than its activity in the methanol system. This suggests that 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 is more suitable for PHE in methanol aqueous solution. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of the catalyst is influenced by the pH of the solution. TEOA (pH = 10.8) and Na2S/Na2SO3 (pH = 12.2) solutions are alkaline, lactic acid (pH = 2.0) solution is acidic, and methanol (pH = 6.5) solution is more neutral. It is evident that the GQDs/NaNbO3 composite exhibits more efficient PHE performance in neutral environments.

The stability of the PHE performance of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 was subsequently evaluated over five cycles of experiments. Notably, the yield of hydrogen in the second reaction was 50% greater than that of the first reaction (Fig. 5(d)). After five cycles of experiment, the catalyst exhibited sustained high catalytic activity, while pure NaNbO3 did not demonstrate a similar phenomenon in the PHE test. To investigate this phenomenon, we first analyzed the surface vacancies of various catalysts using EPR characterization. As shown in Fig. S4(a), after one round of PHE reaction, the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 displayed a significantly enhanced oxygen vacancy signal at g = 2.004. In addition to ESR, we employed XPS to assess changes in the surface oxygen vacancy concentration before and after the first reaction (Fig. S4(b)). The relative oxygen vacancy concentration of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 increased from 19.98% to 21.44% after the first reaction, which aligns with the ESR results. This provides preliminary evidence that the enhanced catalyst activity after the first cycle was related to the increase in oxygen vacancies. Based on the above characterization results, we considered that the formation of dynamic oxygen vacancies in the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst during the PHE process is driven by the rapid transfer of electrons from NaNbO3 to GQDs under light after the loading of GQDs. This transfer created a fast electron transport channel, generating more oxygen vacancies on the NaNbO3 surface. The process activated additional photogenerated electrons, which interacted with lattice oxygen, ultimately forming dynamic oxygen vacancies [25,61]. This was confirmed by ESR spectroscopy, XPS analysis and DFT calculations. Consequently, the hydrogen evolution rate was significantly accelerated during the second cycle. TEM images (Fig. S5) revealed that the microstructure of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 remained stable before and after the fifth reaction, as indicated by the consistent morphology and unchanged lattice fringe spacing compared to the fresh catalyst. XRD results (Fig. S6(a)) showed a slight decrease in crystallinity after the fifth reaction, but the crystalline phases and components remained unchanged. Additionally, XPS analysis was conducted to examine any changes in elemental valence before and after the reaction. The high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S6(b)–(d)) demonstrated that the shapes and compositions of individual elemental peaks remained largely consistent before and after the reaction. However, the detailed spectrum of O 1s revealed that after five rounds of reaction, the concentration of oxygen vacancies increased to 22.38%. This strongly indicated that the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 material exhibited dynamic oxygen vacancies during the PHE process. The structural stability of the catalyst was initially confirmed by both TEM, XRD and XPS analyses.

To further evaluate the catalytic efficiency of the heterojunction, we analyzed the AQE of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst, which exhibited the highest hydrogen production activity at a specific incident wavelength under identical reaction conditions. Since NaNbO3 is the primary component of the composite material, it exhibits measurable AQE in the UV spectrum, reaching 1.0% at 350 nm and gradually decreasing with increasing wavelength, with an AQE of 0.6% at 365 nm. Additionally, a comparison of the PHE performance of NaNbO3-based catalysts reported in the literature with those in this study is presented in Table S2. The PHE activity of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst increased to 1204.9 μmol·g−1·h−1 in the second cycle under methanol as the sacrificial agent, a phenomenon that has not been previously reported in NaNbO3-based catalysts for PHE reactions. The synergistic effect of non-metal modification and dynamic oxygen vacancies significantly enhanced the activity stability of the GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst. This unique finding gives the GQDs/NaNbO3 system a clear advantage.

The photoelectrochemical properties of photocatalysts are critical indicators of carrier separation efficiency and charge transfer during catalytic reactions. To explore the potential reasons for enhanced photocatalytic performance, we conducted TPCD and EIS tests on NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 to assess their charge transfer capabilities, as shown in Fig. 6 . The TPCD curves for GQDs, NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 electrodes are presented in Fig. 6(a). Typically, the photocurrent response of a catalyst in the dark is negligible, and a higher photocurrent density indicated more efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. GQDs exhibited negligible photocurrent response under illumination, and pure NaNbO3 exhibited a weak photocurrent response, reflecting inefficient charge separation. Over five irradiation cycles, 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 demonstrated a significantly enhanced photocurrent density, indicating improved charge separation. Consistent results were obtained from EIS measurements (Fig. 6(b)), where the addition of GQDs reduced the radius of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot, leading to a decrease in charge transfer resistance (Rct) [62]. The Rct of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 catalyst showed a marked decrease compared to pure GQDs and NaNbO3. Additionally, the Rct between the catalyst's VB and the solution likely reflected the impedance from complex interfacial charge transfer between the electrolyte and the catalyst. This suggests that GQD-doped NaNbO3 reduces resistance and enhances charge transfer efficiency, leading to improved photocatalytic performance [63]. Subsequently, PL spectra and TRPL spectra were employed to analyze the capture, recombination, and dynamics of photo-excited charge carriers. Fig. 6(c) compares the PL peak intensities of pure NaNbO3 and 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 under excitation at 360 nm. The results show that the PL intensity of GQDs/NaNbO3 is significantly lower than that of NaNbO3, indicating that the incorporation of GQDs effectively suppresses electron-hole pair recombination and affects the photogenerated charge lifetime. Furthermore, TRPL spectra were used to characterize the lifetime of photogenerated charges. The shorter lifetime (τ1) was attributed to non-radiative carrier recombination, while the longer lifetime (τ2) was attributed to radiative exciton recombination. The average fluorescence lifetime of 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 (2.10 ns) determined using a biexponential decay function (Fig. 6(d)) is shorter compared to that of pristine NaNbO3 (2.33 ns), indicating efficient photogenerated carrier migration and faster participation of photo-generated electrons in the water decomposition process [64,65]. The decrease in PL intensity and shortened lifetime in TRPL spectra demonstrate that the close interaction between GQDs and NaNbO3 facilitates non-radiative transfer of additional charge carriers [66]. The seamless interface introduced by GQDs facilitates the efficient separation and migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs from NaNbO3 to GQDs. This process further accelerates charge separation and enhances participation in catalytic reactions on the catalyst surface. These findings suggest that the introduction of GQDs not only provides abundant reactive sites but also significantly enhances interfacial charge separation and transfer, contributing to improved photocatalytic performance.

To enhance understanding of the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the catalyst, free radical trapping experiments were conducted to identify the primary active species involved in the reaction. A series of active species trapping experiments photocatalytic tests were performed using Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ), and Tert-Butanol (TBA) as trapping agents for holes (h+), superoxide radicals (O2 –), and hydroxyl radicals (OH), respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), these experiments demonstrate the critical role of these active species in the photocatalytic process, each exerting varying degrees of influence on performance. Control experiments with the respective trapping agents demonstrated a significant reduction in activity. Among the species, O2 – had the most pronounced effect, resulting in minimal H2 production during the PHE reaction with BQ.

The active species were further identified using the spin-trapping ESR technique, as shown in Fig. 7(b)–(d). No ESR signals were detected for DMPO−O2 – or DMPO–OH in any of the photocatalysts under dark conditions. However, a distinct 1:1:1 triplet signal was observed for TEMPO–h+ (Fig. 7(b)). After 5 min of illumination, the intensity of the TEMPO–h+ peak in 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 gradually decreased compared to NaNbO3, indicating that the introduction of GQDs enhances electron production [67]. Notably, the characteristic peaks of both DMPO–O2 – and DMPO–OH were detected under light irradiation (Fig. 7(c)–(d)). Furthermore, 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 exhibited significantly higher peak intensities for both DMPO–O2 – and DMPO–OH compared to NaNbO3 [68]. These results suggest that GQDs can effectively enhance the generation of reactive species and further improve the efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation, while maintaining excellent redox capacities for the photogenerated charges.

To better understand the electronic properties and band alignment of GQDs/NaNbO3 and explore its photocatalytic mechanism, we conducted theoretical calculations using DFT with the GGA-PBE approach for both NaNbO3 and GQDs/NaNbO3 composites [69,70]. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the structural models of NaNbO3 and GQDs/NaNbO3 are presented along with their adsorption energies (Eads) for water molecules. As we all know, the adsorption of water molecules on the catalyst surface is crucial for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). As depicted in the Fig. 8(a), the adsorption energy of water on the GQDs/NaNbO3 model (Eads = −1.67 eV) was lower than that on the NaNbO3 model (Eads = −1.23 eV). This result suggested that the introduction of GQDs reduces the adsorption energy of water on the NaNbO3 surface. Additionally, the density of states (DOS) spectrum elucidates the electronic orbital states, as shown in Fig. 8(b). The p-orbitals of oxygen dominated the VB of NaNbO3, while the CB is primarily hybridized by niobium d-orbitals. Upon introducing GQDs, distinct localized defect states form within the bandgap, leading to tail absorption near the optical absorption edge of the composite catalyst. This modification results in a reduction of the bandgap by approximately 0.16 eV (Fig. 8(c)). Additionally, the Nb d-band center of the GQDs/NaNbO3 composite (−0.906 eV) is positioned further away from the Fermi level compared to that of pristine NaNbO3 (−0.804 eV). This downshift in the d-band center reduces the energy barriers for both water adsorption and dissociation, thereby facilitating H* activation and subsequent H2 desorption. These synergistic effects collectively enhance the HER catalytic efficiency [71–73]. This finding further demonstrates that the introduction of GQDs induces changes in the charge distribution of NaNbO3, thereby modulating its electronic structure [74]. These alterations enhance the conductivity of NaNbO3, facilitate electron transport during the HER process, and improve the adsorption capacity of the catalytic sites for reactants. Subsequently, the charge density isosurfaces at the band edges revealed that the charge densities of the VB and CB are spatially separated, with the charge density of the VB predominantly localized around O atoms and the CB localized around Nb atoms (Fig. 8(d–e)) [75]. This spatial redistribution of VB and CB on different atoms effectively facilitates the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The electron localization function (ELF) (Fig. 8(f–g)) illustrates the degree of electron delocalization. After modification with GQDs, the electrons in the Nb-O bonds on the surface of NaNbO3 become more delocalized, primarily due to the increased strength of the covalent bonds. The uniform distribution of elements in the ELF confirms that charge transfer between the GQDs and NaNbO3 interface does not disrupt the overall structure [76]. Furthermore, differential charge density analysis evaluated the electronic density distribution at the interface (Fig. 8(i)). We simulated the electron transfer model for the GQDs/NaNbO3 interface, showing that accumulated electrons (shown in yellow) are concentrated on the surface of GQDs, while depleted electrons (shown in cyan) are distributed on the surface of NaNbO3. GQDs become an active site conducive to *H reduction. After *H adsorption, more electrons cluster tightly around the GQDs and *H, indicating that GQDs/NaNbO3 exhibits stronger charge exchange [77]. Bader charge analysis (Fig. 8(h)) revealed that approximately 0.47 electrons are transferred from the NaNbO3 surface to the GQDs. This further confirmed that the formation of the GQDs/NaNbO3 structure facilitates the migration of electrons from NaNbO3 to the GQDs, thereby promoting the separation and transfer of photo-generated charge carriers. The Gibbs free energy of adsorption of H species (ΔGH*) was used to investigate the HER process of GQDs/NaNbO3. As shown in Fig. 8(j), the ΔGH* of GQDs/NaNbO3 is approximately 0.72 eV, which is closer to zero compared to NaNbO3 (0.87 eV). This suggests that the electron-rich GQDs act as favorable active sites for H* reduction, making the HER pathway (H2O → H* → H2) energetically more favorable. This indicates that the adsorption and desorption kinetics of hydrogen are optimally balanced in this state, promoting favorable reaction kinetics.

Based on the above experimental and characterization results, we propose a plausible mechanism for PHE using GQDs/NaNbO3, as illustrated in Scheme 2 . As a perovskite-structured semiconductor, NaNbO3 exhibits a relatively high light absorption coefficient. However, its wide bandgap limits its light absorption efficiency. The introduction of GQDs narrows the bandgap of NaNbO3, resulting in a more favorable position of its CB. This reduction in bandgap enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improves the photocatalytic driving force. Additionally, The incorporation of GQDs increases the concentration of oxygen vacancies in NaNbO3 [78]. These oxygen vacancies act as efficient charge trapping centers, enhancing electron transport on the surface and within the catalyst. They also facilitate the formation of defect energy levels that can accept electrons excited from the VB, further optimizing charge transport properties and modulating the semiconductor's electronic structure [54,79]. Simultaneously, the functionalization of GQDs also increases the density of active sites on the NaNbO3 surface, promoting more efficient adsorption and activation of reactant molecules, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance. Subsequently, XPS spectra and DFT results indicate that the interface between GQDs and NaNbO3 forms a rapid charge transfer channel, facilitating the transfer of photogenerated electrons from NaNbO3 to GQDs. This process leads to the formation of additional oxygen vacancies on the NaNbO3 surface, enabling the regeneration of oxygen vacancies and thus improving PHE reaction activity and stability. Moreover, GQDs, acting as electron acceptors, effectively capture photogenerated electrons, increasing their participation in the HER and reducing electron-hole recombination. This improves the utilization efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes accumulated on the VB of the catalyst are continuously consumed by methanol, which is oxidized to CO2 and H2O through reactions with O2 − and OH radicals. The synergistic effect between oxygen vacancies and GQDs plays a crucial role in enhancing the PHE activity and stability of NaNbO3.

In summary, we synthesized GQDs/NaNbO3 heterojunctions with dynamic oxygen vacancies using hydrothermal and ultrasonic self-assembly methods. The synergistic effect of oxygen vacancies and GQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen production of NaNbO3 was thoroughly investigated. GQDs were firmly attached to the surface of NaNbO3, forming a stable heterojunction interface. Notably, the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 composite exhibited optimal PHE performance, achieving a hydrogen generation rate of 775.9 μmol·g−1·h−1 under simulated solar irradiation, with methanol as the sacrificial reagent. Cyclic stability tests revealed that under the influence of dynamic oxygen vacancies, the catalyst’s activity increased by approximately 1.5 times (1204.9 μmol⋅g−1⋅h−1) after the first 5 h of reaction and maintained good stability in subsequent cycles. Experimental characterization and DFT calculations attribute the excellent photocatalytic performance of the 0.5%GQDs/NaNbO3 composite to: (I) the introduction of GQDs, which effectively enhances the light absorption of NaNbO3; (II) the efficient accumulation of photogenerated electrons due to dynamic oxygen vacancies; (III) the charge transfer mechanism between GQDs and NaNbO3, which enhances the separation and migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs; and (IV) the synergistic effect of GQDs and dynamic oxygen vacancies, which provides more surface active sites and improves surface reaction kinetics. The formation of dynamic oxygen vacancies offers valuable insights for the design of photocatalysts. This work successfully addresses the inherent challenges of semiconductor photocatalysts, such as light absorption, charge carrier separation, and surface reaction kinetics, offering significant reference and guidance for the design of efficient niobate composite photocatalysts.
